source_paper_title: Stochastic First-Order Methods for Average-Reward Markov Decision Processes
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Li_2025, title={Stochastic First-Order Methods for Average-Reward Markov Decision Processes}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0241}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0241}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Li, Tianjiao and Wu, Feiyang and Lan, Guanghui}, year={2025}, month=nov, pages={3125–3160} }
context:
Page 2, Introduction (discussion of on-policy learning under Markovian noise).

Up to our knowledge, in spite of intensive research effort of on-policy learning (Tsitsiklis and Van Roy, 1999; Yu and Bertsekas, 2009; Zhang et al., 2021b; Mou et al., 2022), one seemly unresolved problem in RL is whether one can design sampling-efficient on-policy learning algorithms for insufficiently random policies to remove the aforementioned problematic assumption (see Remark 1 of Lan (2022)).
title: Design sample-efficient on-policy learning for average-reward MDPs with weak exploration
background_latex:
Consider a finite average-reward Markov decision process (AMDP) with state space \(\mathcal S\), action space \(\mathcal A\), transition kernel \(P(\cdot\mid s,a)\), and bounded one-step cost \(c(s,a)\). A (randomized stationary) policy is \(\pi(a\mid s)\in\Delta_{|\mathcal A|}\). Let \((s_t,a_t)\) be generated by following \(a_t\sim \pi(\cdot\mid s_t)\) and \(s_{t+1}\sim P(\cdot\mid s_t,a_t)\).

The long-run average cost of \(\pi\) is
\[
\rho(\pi)=\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\,\mathbb E_\pi\Big[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} c(s_t,a_t)\Big],
\]
under standard ergodicity/mixing conditions ensuring the limit exists and is independent of the initial state. An \(\varepsilon\)-optimal policy \(\hat\pi\) satisfies \(\mathbb E[\rho(\hat\pi)-\rho(\pi^*)]\le \varepsilon\), where \(\pi^*\in\arg\min_\pi \rho(\pi)\).

In the Markovian (single-trajectory) on-policy setting, the learner can only collect samples along the trajectory induced by the current policy being learned/updated. A common technical assumption used in prior analyses of on-policy actor-critic / temporal-difference style methods is a uniform lower bound on action probabilities (a “sufficiently random” policy condition): there exists \(\underline\pi>0\) such that \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) for all \(s\in\mathcal S\), \(a\in\mathcal A\), for every policy generated by the algorithm. This enforces persistent exploration but can be unrealistic because optimal policies may be deterministic or nearly deterministic, hence may have \(\pi(a\mid s)=0\) for some actions.
problem_definition_latex: Give an on-policy reinforcement learning algorithm for AMDPs in the Markovian (single-trajectory) sampling model that is sampling-efficient (i.e., has a finite-sample complexity guarantee for returning an \(\varepsilon\)-optimal policy) without imposing a uniform lower bound on the action probabilities of the policies generated by the algorithm (i.e., allowing policies that are not “sufficiently random”, potentially with \(\pi(a\mid s)=0\) for some \((s,a)\)).
latex:
Consider a finite average-reward Markov decision process (AMDP) with state space \(\mathcal S\), action space \(\mathcal A\), transition kernel \(P(\cdot\mid s,a)\), and bounded one-step cost \(c(s,a)\). A (randomized stationary) policy is \(\pi(a\mid s)\in\Delta_{|\mathcal A|}\). Let \((s_t,a_t)\) be generated by following \(a_t\sim \pi(\cdot\mid s_t)\) and \(s_{t+1}\sim P(\cdot\mid s_t,a_t)\).

The long-run average cost of \(\pi\) is
\[
\rho(\pi)=\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\,\mathbb E_\pi\Big[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} c(s_t,a_t)\Big],
\]
under standard ergodicity/mixing conditions ensuring the limit exists and is independent of the initial state. An \(\varepsilon\)-optimal policy \(\hat\pi\) satisfies \(\mathbb E[\rho(\hat\pi)-\rho(\pi^*)]\le \varepsilon\), where \(\pi^*\in\arg\min_\pi \rho(\pi)\).

In the Markovian (single-trajectory) on-policy setting, the learner can only collect samples along the trajectory induced by the current policy being learned/updated. A common technical assumption used in prior analyses of on-policy actor-critic / temporal-difference style methods is a uniform lower bound on action probabilities (a “sufficiently random” policy condition): there exists \(\underline\pi>0\) such that \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) for all \(s\in\mathcal S\), \(a\in\mathcal A\), for every policy generated by the algorithm. This enforces persistent exploration but can be unrealistic because optimal policies may be deterministic or nearly deterministic, hence may have \(\pi(a\mid s)=0\) for some actions.

Give an on-policy reinforcement learning algorithm for AMDPs in the Markovian (single-trajectory) sampling model that is sampling-efficient (i.e., has a finite-sample complexity guarantee for returning an \(\varepsilon\)-optimal policy) without imposing a uniform lower bound on the action probabilities of the policies generated by the algorithm (i.e., allowing policies that are not “sufficiently random”, potentially with \(\pi(a\mid s)=0\) for some \((s,a)\)).
location_in_paper: Page 2, Introduction (discussion of on-policy learning under Markovian noise).
open_problem_quote: Up to our knowledge, in spite of intensive research effort of on-policy learning (Tsitsiklis and Van Roy, 1999; Yu and Bertsekas, 2009; Zhang et al., 2021b; Mou et al., 2022), one seemly unresolved problem in RL is whether one can design sampling-efficient on-policy learning algorithms for insufficiently random policies to remove the aforementioned problematic assumption (see Remark 1 of Lan (2022)).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is existential (design an algorithm) rather than a universal inequality where a single finite instance would refute it. While lower-bound counterexamples might exist, systematically searching for them would require formalizing a specific algorithm class or conjectured rate, which the paper does not specify.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize candidate on-policy exploration mechanisms (e.g., perturbations, scheduling rules, or optimistic bonuses) and evaluate them via simulation-based proxies for sample efficiency on benchmark AMDP families. However, turning empirical performance into a faithful proxy for worst-case finite-sample guarantees is indirect, so automated search would only provide heuristic evidence or candidate algorithmic ideas rather than a definitive resolution.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Li, Wu, and Lan (2024) frame the open problem sharply: in average-reward MDPs, on-policy actor–critic methods become hard to analyze when the evolving policy approaches determinism, because standard TD/critic analyses typically require a uniform lower bound \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) to guarantee that all state–action pairs are sampled often enough. Their paper makes a notable step by introducing EVRTD, which evaluates an arbitrary target policy \(\pi\) by intermittently sampling actions from a perturbed policy \(\tilde\pi\) while keeping the state distribution close to the stationary distribution of \(\pi\). This yields an on-policy-style critic that can handle \(\pi(a\mid s)=0\) and, when coupled with their average-reward SPMD actor, leads to \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) sample complexity in the ergodic Markovian model (up to mixing/feature constants). However, EVRTD still injects explicit exploration through \(\tilde\pi\), so it does not fully realize “weak exploration” in the sense of learning purely from the trajectory of the current policy without any deliberate perturbation.

Among forward citations, the closest conceptual progress on eliminating explicit action-space exploration is in discounted settings: “Policy mirror descent inherently explores action space” proves that discounted SPMD can achieve near-optimal \(\tilde O(1/\varepsilon^2)\) sample complexity without enforcing \(\min_{s,a}\pi(a\mid s)\) lower bounds, via evaluation operators (VBE/TOMC) that control bias/variance and reveal implicit exploration properties of mirror descent. “Auto-exploration for online reinforcement learning” similarly removes explicit exploration floors for discounted online RL under an exploring-optimal-policy structural condition. These works suggest that the actor dynamics plus carefully designed on-policy evaluation may suffice to guarantee adequate coverage, but they do not yet deliver an average-reward analogue.

Within average-reward on-policy learning, recent regret analyses in unichain settings (e.g., “Regret analysis of average-reward unichain mdps via an actor-critic approach” and “Regret Analysis of Unichain Average Reward Constrained MDPs with General Parameterization”) provide finite-time guarantees under weaker dynamical assumptions than uniform ergodicity, but they typically assume bounded score functions and uniform Fisher non-degeneracy, which effectively keep policies in the interior of the simplex and thus do not allow true zeros. Other average-reward advances without action-probability floors tend to be value-based or model-based (e.g., optimistic Q-learning regret bounds, or online PAC identification via DFE), rather than on-policy policy-gradient/actor–critic methods.

Overall, the problem remains open in its strongest form: obtaining a finite-sample \(\varepsilon\)-optimality guarantee for a genuinely on-policy (single-trajectory) average-reward control algorithm that neither enforces nor injects any explicit exploration mechanism and still allows \(\pi(a\mid s)=0\) along the iterates. Promising directions include adapting the discounted “inherent exploration” analyses to the average-reward Poisson-equation/differential-value setting, and developing critic estimators whose stability does not rely on full-support visitation but instead exploits structural properties (e.g., exploring optimal policies, unichain hitting-time controls, or implicit regularization from mirror maps) to guarantee sufficient coverage of the relevant state–action support.

sources.title: Policy mirror descent inherently explores action space
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves that an online (single-trajectory) policy gradient method—stochastic policy mirror descent (SPMD)—can achieve near-optimal sample complexity without any explicit action-space exploration (no enforced lower bound on \(\pi(a\mid s)\)). For discounted finite MDPs, with uniformly mixing state chains (Assumption 1), SPMD with the proposed value-based estimation (VBE) operator and KL divergence attains \(\tilde O(1/\varepsilon^2)\) samples to return an \(\varepsilon\)-optimal policy in expectation (Theorem 4.1). With the truncated on-policy Monte Carlo (TOMC) operator, SPMD attains \(\tilde O(H_D/\varepsilon^2)\) samples with high probability (Theorem 5.2), where \(H_D\) depends on the effective horizon and chosen Bregman divergence (e.g., polynomial for Tsallis divergence).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a sample-efficient on-policy algorithm for average-reward MDPs that does not require a uniform lower bound on action probabilities. This paper directly targets the analogous exploration issue (avoiding assumptions like \(\min_a \pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\)) and provides on-policy single-trajectory policy-gradient methods with \(\tilde O(1/\varepsilon^2)\) sample complexity without explicit action exploration. However, the results are for discounted MDPs (objective \(\mathbb E_\vartheta[V^\pi]\) with \(\gamma\in(0,1)\)), not the average-reward criterion \(\rho(\pi)\). Thus it does not solve the AMDP open problem as stated, but offers techniques (VBE/TOMC evaluation, inherent exploration via truncation, accumulated-noise control) that are plausibly adaptable to average-reward settings.

sources.title: Regret analysis of average-reward unichain mdps via an actor-critic approach
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes an on-policy natural actor–critic algorithm with batching (NAC-B) for finite average-reward MDPs under the unichain assumption (single recurrent class, allowing transient states and periodicity). Under Assumptions 1–4, with total trajectory length 
\(T=2KHB\), choosing \(K=\Theta(\sqrt{T}/\log T)\), \(B=\Theta(\sqrt{T})\), \(H=\Theta(\log T)\), Theorem 1 proves an expected regret bound
\[\mathbb E[\mathrm{Reg}_T]\le \tilde O\big(T(\sqrt{\epsilon_{\mathrm{bias}}}+\sqrt{\epsilon_{\mathrm{app}}})+\sqrt{T}(C^2C_{\mathrm{tar}}+C_{\mathrm{tar}})\big),\]
where \(\epsilon_{\mathrm{bias}}\) is policy-class approximation bias, \(\epsilon_{\mathrm{app}}\) is critic approximation error, and \(C=C_{\mathrm{hit}}+C_{\mathrm{tar}}\) are unichain hitting/target-time constants controlling Cesàro mixing.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a sample-efficient on-policy algorithm for average-reward MDPs that avoids imposing a uniform lower bound \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) on all actions (i.e., allows near-deterministic policies). NAC-B is on-policy and average-reward, and its analysis explicitly targets settings beyond ergodicity (unichain, possibly periodic/transient). However, the paper still assumes (i) a Fisher non-degeneracy condition \(F(\theta)\succeq \mu I\) uniformly (Assumption 4) and (ii) bounded score \(\|\nabla_\theta\log\pi_\theta(a\mid s)\|\le G_1\) (Assumption 3), which typically exclude policies with \(\pi_\theta(a\mid s)=0\) (since \(\log\pi\) and its gradient blow up) and thus act as an implicit “no-vanishing-probabilities”/interior-of-simplex requirement. Therefore it does not fully resolve the open problem as stated, but it is strong partial progress: it provides finite-time on-policy guarantees in a weak exploration/mixing regime (unichain, no ergodicity), and introduces batching/Cesàro-mixing tools that could be adapted to handle weaker exploration assumptions.

sources.title: Regret Analysis of Unichain Average Reward Constrained MDPs with General Parameterization
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes PDNAC-BI, a primal–dual natural actor–critic algorithm for infinite-horizon average-reward constrained MDPs under the unichain assumption and general policy parameterization, using MLMC estimators and an explicit burn-in. Main guarantee (Theorem 9): with loop sizes 
- inner steps per epoch: \(H=\Theta(\log T)\), epochs \(K=\Theta(T/\log T)\), step sizes \(\alpha,\beta=\Theta(1/\sqrt{T})\),
under assumptions including unichain dynamics, Slater condition, critic matrix lower bounds, bounded/Lipschitz score, and Fisher non-degeneracy, the expected regret and cumulative constraint violation satisfy
\[\mathbb E[\mathrm{Reg}_T]=\tilde O\big(T(\sqrt{\epsilon_{\mathrm{bias}}}+\sqrt{\epsilon_{\mathrm{app}}})+ C_{\mathrm{tar}}^2 C\sqrt{T}\big),\qquad \mathbb E\big[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} J_c(\theta_t)\big]=\tilde O\big(T(\sqrt{\epsilon_{\mathrm{bias}}}+\sqrt{\epsilon_{\mathrm{app}}})+ C_{\mathrm{tar}}^2 C\sqrt{T}\big).\]
Here \(C= C_{\mathrm{hit}}+C_{\mathrm{tar}}\) are unichain complexity constants and \(\epsilon_{\mathrm{bias}},\epsilon_{\mathrm{app}}\) are actor/critic approximation errors.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a sample-efficient on-policy algorithm for average-reward MDPs without enforcing a uniform lower bound \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) (i.e., allowing near-deterministic policies). This paper is on-policy and average-reward, and it explicitly relaxes ergodicity/mixing-time assumptions to the weaker unichain setting using MLMC and logarithmic burn-in, giving finite-time \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\)-type regret bounds.

However, it does not directly remove the need for persistent exploration in the sense of allowing policies with exact zeros in action probabilities while maintaining the required smoothness/invertibility assumptions: it assumes bounded/Lipschitz score functions and, crucially, Fisher non-degeneracy \(F(\theta)\succeq \mu I\) uniformly over \(\Theta\). For common parameterizations (e.g., softmax), \(F(\theta)\) becomes singular as action probabilities approach 0, so this assumption typically rules out truly deterministic/zero-probability actions unless additional mechanisms (e.g., clipping, enforced stochasticity) are used. Thus the paper provides partial progress (finite-time on-policy guarantees in a weaker dynamical regime) but does not fully resolve the “weak exploration / no uniform lower bound” aspect of the open problem.

sources.title: Sharper model-free reinforcement learning for average-reward markov decision processes
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives provably efficient tabular model-free algorithms for average-reward MDPs under weakly communicating assumptions. In the online (single-trajectory) setting, Algorithm 1 (UCB-AVG) achieves high-probability regret
\[R(T)=\tilde O\big(S^5A^2\,\mathrm{sp}(h^*)\,\sqrt T\big).\]
In the simulator (generative model) setting, Algorithm 6 finds an \(\varepsilon\)-optimal policy with sample complexity
\[\tilde O\Big( SA\,\mathrm{sp}^2(h^*)\,\varepsilon^{-2} + S^2A\,\mathrm{sp}(h^*)\,\varepsilon^{-1}\Big).\]
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an on-policy learning algorithm for average-reward MDPs that is sample-efficient without enforcing a uniform lower bound \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) on action probabilities of the policies generated (i.e., allowing near-deterministic policies). This paper studies model-free RL for average-reward MDPs and crucially works under the weakly communicating assumption (no uniform mixing / uniform exploration condition). However, its main online guarantee is a regret bound for an optimistic Q-learning style algorithm (UCB-AVG) that is not an on-policy actor-critic/TD policy-gradient method; it uses optimism-driven exploration and greedy action selection, rather than updating a stochastic policy that could become deterministic. The paper does not explicitly address or remove the \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) assumption in analyses of on-policy policy-gradient/actor-critic methods, nor does it provide a finite-sample PAC guarantee for an on-policy policy-iteration/actor-critic algorithm without such lower bounds. Thus it is relevant as partial progress toward sample-efficient single-trajectory learning in AMDPs without strong exploration/mixing assumptions, but it does not directly solve the stated open problem in the on-policy policy-learning sense.

sources.title: Finding good policies in average-reward Markov Decision Processes without prior knowledge
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives (ε,δ)-PAC best-policy identification algorithms for average-reward MDPs without prior knowledge of problem-dependent parameters: in the generative-model setting, Diameter Free Exploration (DFE) is (ε,δ)-PAC with sample complexity \(\tilde O\big((SAD/\varepsilon^2)\log(1/\delta) + D^2SA\log(1/\delta)+D^2S^2A\big)\) (Theorem 2); in the online (single-trajectory) setting, an Online-DFE variant is (ε,δ)-PAC with sample complexity \(\bar O_\delta(SAD^2/\varepsilon^2 + S^2AD^3)\) (Theorem 4). It also proves hardness results: estimating the optimal bias span \(H\) can require arbitrarily many samples even with a generative model (Theorem 1), and no online PAC algorithm can have sample complexity polynomial in \(S,A,H\) in general (Theorem 3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a sample-efficient on-policy (single-trajectory) algorithm for average-reward MDPs that does not require a uniform lower bound \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) on action probabilities (i.e., allows near-deterministic policies). This paper studies PAC best-policy identification in average-reward MDPs, including an online (single-trajectory) setting, and provides an online PAC algorithm (Online-DFE) with finite-sample guarantees. However, the algorithmic framework is model-based exploration/collection (via diameter estimation and uniform sampling converted to online using GOSPRL), not an on-policy actor-critic/TD method, and it does not explicitly address or remove the “sufficiently random policy” assumption for policies generated by gradient-based on-policy methods. It does, nonetheless, demonstrate that PAC identification is possible online without assuming persistent randomization of the executed policy, and it provides lower bounds clarifying what parameter scalings are impossible online.

sources.title: Policy Gradient Algorithms in Average-Reward Multichain MDPs
sources.theorem_or_result:
They establish convergence and finite-sample complexity guarantees for an on-policy policy-gradient-type method in average-reward multichain MDPs, but only over the restricted class of strictly positive (or b1-clipped) policies. Concretely, for b1-clipped policy mirror ascent with c0_k\in \Pi_\alpha:=\{\pi: \pi(a\mid s)\ge \alpha\}, they prove monotone improvement and rates such as
- (Theorem 9) \(J^{\pi_\alpha}_\mu- J^{\pi_k}_\mu \le \frac{1}{k+1}\big( D^{\rho^{\pi_\alpha}_\mu}(\pi_\alpha,\pi_0)/\eta + C_\alpha(J^{\pi_\alpha}_\mu-J^{\pi_0}_\mu)\big)\).
- (Theorem 13) Under a generative model, stochastic b1-clipped policy mirror ascent returns an \(\epsilon\)-optimal policy w.r.t. \(\Pi_\alpha\) with explicit sample complexity bounds (polynomial in \(|\mathcal S||\mathcal A|\), \(1/\epsilon\), and problem-dependent quantities).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a sample-efficient on-policy algorithm for average-reward MDPs in the single-trajectory (Markovian) sampling model without enforcing a uniform lower bound \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\). This paper explicitly restricts its main algorithmic analysis to \(\Pi_\alpha\subset \Pi_+\), i.e., it enforces a uniform exploration floor \(\alpha>0\) (Algorithm 1/3). Their guarantees are for \(\epsilon\)-optimality with respect to positive policies (and b1-clipped policies), not for potentially deterministic optimal policies with zeros. They do discuss letting \(\alpha\to 0\) and, in weakly communicating MDPs (Appendix E), relate \(J^*_\mu\) and \(J^{*}_{+,\mu}\), but the algorithm still maintains \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \alpha\) during learning. Moreover, their sample-complexity results are primarily for a generative model; the open problem is specifically the Markovian single-trajectory on-policy setting. Thus the paper advances theory for average-reward policy gradients and provides sample complexity under stronger exploration and sampling assumptions, but does not remove the uniform lower-bound requirement in the on-policy trajectory model.

sources.title: Policy Mirror Descent with Temporal Difference Learning: Sample Complexity under Online Markov Data
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves finite-sample complexity guarantees for tabular policy mirror descent with temporal-difference critics under online Markov data in discounted MDPs. In particular, for Expected TD-PMD (Algorithm 1) and Approximate TD-PMD (Algorithm 3), under ergodicity/mixing and a sufficiently exploratory behavior policy \(\pi_b\) with \(\min_{s,a}\pi_b(a\mid s)>0\), it establishes \(\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) sample complexity for average-time \(\varepsilon\)-optimality with constant policy steps (Theorems 3.13, 4.11), and \(O(\varepsilon^{-2})\) sample complexity for last-iterate \(\varepsilon\)-optimality with adaptive policy steps and adaptive batch sizes (Theorems 3.17, 4.15), measured in \(\mathbb E\|Q^*-Q^{\pi_K}\|_\infty\) (and hence value suboptimality).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a sample-efficient on-policy algorithm for average-reward MDPs under single-trajectory data without enforcing a uniform lower bound \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) on the learned policies (allowing zeros). This paper does not address that setting: (i) it studies discounted MDPs (objective \(V^\pi\) with \(\gamma<1\)), not average-reward; (ii) its Markov-data algorithms are off-policy or mixed-policy and rely on a separate behavior policy \(\pi_b\) that must satisfy \(\min_{s,a}\pi_b(a\mid s)>0\) (Assumption 3.1), i.e., a strong exploration condition—though imposed on \(\pi_b\), not on the iterates \(\pi_k\); (iii) it does allow the target/learned policies \(\pi_k\) to become greedy/deterministic (even \(\pi_k(a\mid s)=0\)) via large/adaptive mirror-descent steps, but the guarantees still require persistent exploration through \(\pi_b\). Thus it provides partial progress/related insight on removing lower bounds on the *learned* policy, but it does not solve the on-policy average-reward weak-exploration problem.

sources.title: Auto-exploration for online reinforcement learning
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper develops “auto-exploration” variants of stochastic policy mirror descent (SPMD) for finite discounted MDPs in the online (single-trajectory) model. Under Assumption 3.2 (existence of a deterministic optimal policy \(\pi^*\) whose induced Markov chain is irreducible with stationary distribution bounded below and geometric mixing), it proves a high-probability last-iterate guarantee and an algorithm-independent sample complexity bound: in the tabular case (Theorem 3.5), with probability \(1-\delta\), \(V^{\pi_k}(s)-V^{\pi^*}(s)\le \epsilon\,\log(4k)\log\big(\tfrac{2|S|\log(4k)}{\delta}\big)\) for all \(s\), using total samples \(\tilde O(D_{\mathrm{expl}}(\delta)\,\epsilon^{-2})\) where \(D_{\mathrm{expl}}(\delta)\) is algorithm-independent. It also gives a linear-function-approximation variant (Theorem 4.12) with analogous \(\tilde O(D_{\mathrm{expl}}(\delta)\,\epsilon^{-2})\) dependence but with additional approximation error terms and explicit exploration components.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a sample-efficient on-policy algorithm for average-reward MDPs without imposing a uniform lower bound \(\pi(a\mid s)\ge \underline\pi\) on all intermediate policies. This paper directly targets the *discounted* infinite-horizon setting, not average-reward, and its main results are for discounted objectives \(V^\pi\) with discount \(\gamma\). It explicitly discusses (Conclusion) that extending to average-reward via discount-to-average reductions would yield suboptimal, algorithm-dependent \(O(\epsilon^{-7})\) complexity and leaves a tailored average-reward design to future work. Nonetheless, it is relevant because it removes the need for a uniform action-probability lower bound in the discounted on-policy setting by leveraging an “exploring optimal policy” structural assumption and implicit exploration (tabular case), plus parameter-free dynamic mixing-time ideas and robust estimation—techniques that could inform an average-reward analogue.
